question: What is the purpose of the <footer> element?
options: To represent the footer of a page or section
To mark up the main content
explanation: <footer> represents the footer of its nearest ancestor sectioning content or sectioning root element.

question: Can a <footer> be placed inside an <article>?
options: Only if the article has no header
explanation: <footer> can be used inside <article> for article-specific information like author, date, and tags.

question: What is the implicit ARIA role of a page-level <footer>?
explanation: When <footer> is a page-level footer (not nested in article/section), it has an implicit role of 'contentinfo'.

question: Which element can a <footer> NOT contain?
explanation: A <footer> must not contain <header>, <main>, or another <footer>.

question: Is a <footer> inside an <article> considered a landmark?
options: No, only page-level footers have the contentinfo role
Only if it has an aria-label
explanation: When <footer> is inside <article>, <section>, or <aside>, it does NOT have a landmark role.

question: What element should you use inside <footer> for contact information?
explanation: The <address> element provides contact information for the nearest <article> or <body>.

question: Can a <footer> contain a <section>?
options: Only if the section has a heading
explanation: <footer> can contain <section> elements to organize footer content.

question: What is the default display of <footer>?
explanation: <footer> is a block-level element by default.

question: Can a page have multiple <footer> elements?
options: Yes, one per sectioning root or content
explanation: A page can have multiple <footer> elements — one as the page footer and others inside articles or sections.

question: Which of the following is appropriate content for a <footer>?
explanation: Copyright notices, legal links, and related metadata are appropriate footer content.

question: What is the difference between <footer> and <div>?
explanation: <footer> carries semantic meaning of a footer region. <div> is a generic container.

question: Should you put <footer> inside or outside <main>?
explanation: The page-level <footer> should be outside <main>. Section-level footers can be inside <main> if nested in articles.

question: What attribute can give a <footer> an accessible name?
explanation: Use aria-label or aria-labelledby to provide an accessible name for the footer landmark.

question: What is the purpose of the <address> element in a footer?
options: To add a physical address only
To mark up any postal address
explanation: The <address> element provides contact information for the nearest article or body.

question: Can <footer> contain <h1>-<h6> headings?
options: Yes, for section headings within the footer
No, headings are not allowed in footer
explanation: <footer> can contain heading elements for organizing footer content sections.

question: What happens to the contentinfo role when <footer> is inside a <section>?
options: It loses the contentinfo landmark role
explanation: When <footer> is nested in a sectioning element, it does NOT have the contentinfo landmark role.

question: Which of these is invalid nesting?
explanation: A <footer> cannot contain another <footer>. This is invalid HTML.

question: What styling pattern is commonly used for multi-column footers?
explanation: CSS Grid and Flexbox are the modern approaches for multi-column footer layouts.

question: What is the semantic way to mark up copyright information in a footer?
explanation:
Both

and are semantically appropriate for copyright text. represents fine print or legal text.

question: Should a page-level footer have an accessible name?
options: Only if there are multiple footers
Only if it contains a navigation
explanation: Providing an accessible name for the page-level footer helps screen reader users distinguish it from other landmarks.
